name: Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF) — Regular Army
detail: SAF regular army estimated at 100,000–200,000 personnel with armor, artillery, and infantry units. Based primarily in Port Sudan, Kordofan, and Nile River Valley. Led by Gen. Abdel Fattah al-Burhan. Recaptured Khartoum, Omdurman, Bahri, Wad Madani, and El Obeid in 2024-2025 counteroffensive.

name: Sudan Air Force (SAF)
detail: Operates MiG-29 fighters, Su-25 attack jets, Mi-24 helicopter gunships, and An-32 transport aircraft. Conducted sustained airstrikes on RSF positions in Khartoum, Omdurman, and Darfur throughout the war. Supplemented by Iranian-supplied Shahed-type drones from 2024. Based at Merowe and Port Sudan air bases.

name: Bayraktar TB2 (Turkish-supplied, SAF)
detail: Turkey supplied SAF with Bayraktar TB2 armed drones, which were used in strike operations against RSF positions. TB2 drone strikes were reported in Omdurman, Khartoum, and South Darfur operations. Turkish military cooperation with SAF represented one of the most significant foreign interventions supporting Sudan's recognized government.

name: Iranian Shahed-Type Drones (SAF)
detail: SAF reportedly received Iranian Shahed loitering munitions and other drone systems from late 2023 onward. Iranian-supplied drones significantly enhanced SAF precision strike capability against RSF concentrations. The supply pattern mirrors Iran's drone transfers to Russia in Ukraine. Both parties officially denied the supply arrangement.

name: Rapid Support Forces (RSF) — Main Force
detail: RSF estimated at 70,000–100,000 fighters, predominantly light infantry with technicals (armed pickup trucks), anti-aircraft guns, and some armor. Built from former Janjaweed militias. Excels at rapid movement and urban warfare. Controlled most of Khartoum from April 2023 until January 2025. Controls all of Darfur.

name: RSF-Aligned Arab Militias (Darfur)
detail: RSF augmented by Arab tribal militias in Darfur, particularly in West and South Darfur. These allied fighters were directly implicated in the Massalit ethnic cleansing in El Geneina and the Ardamata massacre. The militias operated with significant autonomy under an RSF umbrella, conducting ethnically targeted raids on non-Arab communities.

name: RSF Technical Fleet (Armed Pickup Trucks)
detail: RSF deployed thousands of armed Toyota Land Cruiser-type pickup trucks ('technicals') mounting 23mm/14.5mm anti-aircraft guns, heavy machine guns, and rocket launchers. This mobile strike capability was central to RSF rapid advance tactics across flat Darfur and Khartoum terrain. Vehicles were photographed bearing UAE-origin markings.

name: Sudan Liberation Army — Minnawi (SLA-MM)
detail: Armed Zaghawa-led movement led by North Darfur Governor Minni Minnawi, aligned with SAF against RSF. SLA-MM fighters contributed to El Fasher's defense during the 18-month RSF siege. Minnawi organized Darfuri non-Arab resistance against RSF ethnic cleansing in North Darfur.

name: Justice and Equality Movement (JEM)
detail: JEM, led by Finance Minister Jibril Ibrahim, is an armed Darfuri movement allied with SAF. JEM fighters participated in the defense of El Fasher and North Darfur alongside SAF and SLA-MM. JEM has historical presence in Chad and cross-border supply relationships with Chadian authorities.

name: Egyptian Military — SAF Ally
detail: Egypt provided direct military support to SAF including weapon systems, drones, and logistics. Egyptian forces maintained presence along the Sudan-Egypt border in the Halayib triangle area. Egypt views SAF as a strategic partner and the RSF-Egypt relationship as adversarial given Egypt's concerns about Nile water security and its hostility toward UAE-aligned forces.

name: UAE — RSF Financial & Weapons Backer
detail: UN Security Council sanctions monitors named the UAE in 2024 as providing weapons, financial support, and logistical assistance to the RSF. RSF gold exports worth $860M+ annually pass primarily through UAE channels. Sudan severed diplomatic ties with UAE in 2024, citing UAE's role in enabling RSF atrocities. UAE denied the allegations.

name: Al Junaid Multi-Activities Corporation (RSF)
detail: RSF's primary economic arm, controlling major gold-producing areas in Darfur including Jebel Amer mines. Produces an estimated $860M in annual gold revenue, of which 50-80% is smuggled to the UAE. Al Junaid finances RSF salaries, weapons, vehicles, and operations, making RSF financially independent from Sudan's state budget.

name: Defence Industries System (SAF)
detail: SAF military-economic conglomerate running 200+ companies across mining, telecommunications, agriculture, and import/export — generating estimated $2B+ in annual revenue. Controls significant parts of Sudan's formal economy. In 2019, the SAF's enterprises absorbed 82% of Sudan's state budget resources. The DIS provides SAF with economic independence from civilian oversight.

name: SPLM-N (al-Hilu faction) — RSF Ally
detail: Sudan People's Liberation Movement-North led by Abdel Aziz al-Hilu, the South Kordofan rebel movement, entered a battlefield alliance with the RSF in early 2026. The RSF-SPLM-N alliance captured Kurmuk (Blue Nile State) on March 23, 2026, staging from Ethiopia, and is pushing toward Ed Damazin and the Roseires Dam. The alliance represents a major expansion of the anti-SAF coalition beyond Darfur and Kordofan.
